title: How Trauma Creates Disconnection from Hunger and Fullness
researcherTopic: radical acceptance, RAIN technique, Buddhist psychology
metaDescription: Trauma can sever the connection to your body's signals of hunger and fullness. Learn why this happens and how to gently rebuild interoceptive awareness.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle hands hold a simple, empty ceramic bowl. Warm, luminous morning light streams in from a nearby window, casting soft shadows and illuminating the subtle textures of the handmade pottery. The scene evokes a sense of quiet contemplation, nourishment, and the safety of a new beginning.
finalH2: The Slow Return to Embodied Trust
htmlContent:
Tara Brach, in her work on radical acceptance, often speaks of the trance of unworthiness, a state where we disconnect from our own lived experience - a disconnection that frequently begins not in the mind, but in the body's most fundamental signals, like the simple call of hunger or the quiet whisper of being full. This isn't a conscious choice, but a deeply ingrained survival strategy, a way the nervous system learns to work through a world that felt unsafe, unpredictable, or overwhelming. The language of our own biology becomes foreign, a dialect we were forced to forget in order to endure.

The Body as a Forgotten Language

Interoception is the technical term for this internal language, the complex network of signals that allows us to feel and interpret our body's inner state - our heart rate, our breath, the need for rest, and, most primally, our hunger and satiety. It is the foundation of our felt sense of self, the very bedrock of our emotional and physical experience. But when trauma enters the equation, particularly the developmental and relational trauma that defines C-PTSD, this entire system is thrown into disarray. The body, instead of being a source of reliable information and safety, becomes a field of potential threats.

When a child's environment is characterized by neglect, chaos, or abuse, the body's signals become entangled with fear, anxiety, and the desperate need for safety. A pang of hunger might be followed not by a warm meal, but by the terrifying sound of a parent's angry footsteps, or the deep emptiness of being alone. The body learns that its needs are not only unmet but potentially dangerous to express. In order to survive, the developing nervous system makes a brilliant, albeit costly, adaptation: it begins to mute these internal signals. If feeling your body means feeling terror, then the most intelligent thing to do is to stop feeling your body. We retreat into the attic of the intellect, becoming observers of our lives rather than participants, all the while wondering why we feel so disconnected from ourselves and the world. The question of what interoceptive awareness truly means becomes a distant academic inquiry rather than a lived reality.

Hunger and Fullness: More Than Just Fuel Gauges

In a regulated nervous system, hunger and fullness are simple, elegant signals - the body's way of maintaining homeostasis. They are the biological equivalents of a fuel gauge, indicating a need for energy or signaling that the tank is full. But through the lens of trauma, these signals are stripped of their neutrality. Hunger ceases to be a simple request for food; it can become a terrifying echo of real neglect, a reminder of a time when needs were a source of danger or abandonment. Fullness is no longer a gentle sense of satisfaction; it might feel like a loss of control, a vulnerability, or a trigger for deep-seated shame about taking up space.

This is because for a developing human, the experience of being fed is inextricably linked with the experience of being safe, seen, and soothed. The process of attunement, where a caregiver accurately perceives and responds to a child's needs, is the foundation of a secure attachment and a regulated nervous system. When this process is disrupted - when a cry of hunger is met with anger, indifference, or inconsistency... the child's brain learns a devastating equation: my needs are a threat to my connection with my caregiver, and therefore a threat to my survival. It learns to predict danger, not nourishment. It anticipates abandonment, not attunement. This is the cruel genius of adaptation. The body does what it must to survive the unsurvivable, even if it means sacrificing its own internal compass.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

The Spectrum of Disconnection: From Numbness to Noise

This disconnection from hunger and fullness doesn't sign in a single, uniform way; it exists on a spectrum, often oscillating between two poles: somatic numbness and overwhelming noise. On one end of the spectrum is a deep numbness, an inability to feel or interpret the body's signals at all. What emerges is the person who can go an entire day without realizing they haven't eaten, only to be hit with a sudden wave of dizziness or irritability. The signals of hunger are so faint they are easily ignored, overridden by the demands of work, caregiving, or the simple, consuming task of getting through the day. This numbness is not a personal failing but a deeply learned defense, a form of dissociation that allowed the self to endure a reality where bodily needs were irrelevant or dangerous. It is a fortress built to protect from overwhelming sensation, but its walls are so thick that even the most basic, life-sustaining messages cannot get through.

Seriously.

The Architecture of Survival: Why the Body Adapts This Way

This process requires us to gently challenge the very identity structures that were built around this disconnection. For many, the identity of being someone who is "in control," "unflappable," or "low-maintenance" is a direct result of this somatic muting. It is a work into the unknown, a willingness to let go of the familiar armor of the intellect and to step into the messy, unpredictable, and genuinely alive world of sensation. We must be willing to look beyond the surface-level explanations and behaviors, because often, the most layered defenses are the ones that keep us stuck in familiar loops of suffering.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link).

Re-establishing the Connection: A Practice of Gentle Inquiry

So how does one begin to rebuild a bridge to a continent that has been submerged for decades? The answer, frustratingly and beautifully, is slowly, and with a quality of attention that is radically different from the problem-solving mindset that dominates our culture. The impulse is to want to "fix" the disconnection, to find the right technique or protocol that will restore perfect interoceptive awareness. The path back to the body is not one of conquest, but of courtship. It is a practice of gentle, non-demand inquiry, a willingness to listen to the silence and the noise without needing either to change.

The aim is not to achieve some mythical state of perfect attunement where every bodily signal is received with crystal clarity. That is a goal born of the same perfectionism that often fuels the trauma response itself. The true practice is simply to cultivate the willingness to listen at all. We are not trying to manage the body; we are learning to be with it. That becomes the heart of the practice of gentle inquiry, a process that honors the body's wisdom, even when that wisdom speaks in whispers, riddles, or roars.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

The Slow Return to Embodied Trust

It is crucial to hold this entire process with a significant sense of patience, recognizing that the return to embodied trust is not a linear progression but a slow, meandering spiral. There will be days of clarity, where the signals of hunger and fullness feel distinct and reliable, followed by days or weeks where the old numbness or noise returns with a vengeance. We are not simply learning a new skill; we are at its core rewiring a nervous system that has been shaped by years, or even decades, of perceived threat. We are rebuilding a relationship with a part of ourselves that has been exiled, and relationships are never built on a predictable timeline.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

What happens next is not up to the research, or the framework, or the therapist, or this article. What happens next is up to the person reading this sentence right now. The only thing missing is the decision.

Wild, right?

Frequently Asked Questions

Why can't I just "listen to my body" like everyone says?

For individuals with a history of complex trauma, the channels of communication with the body have often been intentionally severed as a survival mechanism. The invitation to simply "listen" can feel invalidating because it overlooks the reality that the signals may be muted, chaotic, or encoded with danger. The work is not just to listen, but to first create the conditions of safety and presence that allow the body's true voice to emerge from behind decades of protective silence or noise.

Is it possible to fully heal this disconnection?

Healing this disconnection is a genuine possibility, but it may not look like a return to a state that existed before the trauma; rather, it is the creation of a new, more conscious relationship with the body. It involves moving from a state of unconscious disconnection to one of conscious connection, characterized by patience, curiosity, and a deep respect for the body's adaptive wisdom. Full healing may not mean the complete absence of old patterns, but the presence of a compassionate awareness that knows how to move through them when they arise.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

The path back to the body is not a gentle return to a safe harbor; it is the deliberate act of building a new harbor, plank by plank, in the midst of an ocean that is, and has always been, in motion. The ultimate challenge is not to finally understand your hunger, but to ask if you are willing to sit with the striking discomfort of not knowing, and to trust that a new kind of knowing can emerge from that very space.
